sentences.text: My name is Anna Perl, and I was born Anna Duklauer.
I was born in Szczawnica, Poland.
It was a very nice little town.
And was there seven mineral waters.
And people from all over came.
And they-- it was mineral water.
And they said that the water was healthy.
So with tuberculosis, asthma, people came there, mostly during the summer.
It was very busy.
Was a very nice place.
But my father thought it's not big enough, and he can make better living in a bigger city.
So we moved to Zakopane.
He was a tailor.
But in Zakopane, he built a little business.
It was souvenir business and leather.
They-- they paint with colors the leather.
And they make albums, wallets, and cover books.
And this was his business.
He just cut out.
And he has women, and they do this for him.
But in 1939 broke out the war, September the 1st.
And they came in, the German.
And I don't know how they knew.
They know where he has all this stuff, the leather stuff, and they took everything from him.
Very nice.
My parents, they'd been very good parents.
Very good marriage, the best one I know.
And my father, first he was a tailor.
Then he was a tailor and the business he has.
And my mother, she took care of us, we being three children, two girls and one boy.
And when she had a little time, so she helped my father.
She-- she-- in sewing, she could help him in everywhere.
And this was life like until 1939, when the war break-- broke out.
My mother, she was a very religious woman.
And she came from a very religious home.
And every Friday evening, she light the candles.
And High Holy Days we observed.
My father, he wasn't religious.
But it was freedom in our family.
Everybody lived how-- how he want to live.
And they didn't influence us, no father on his side, no mother on her side.
Just they wait until we grow up and make up our minds.
And--
Yeah.
Yeah, we went, but only on High Holy Days we went to synagogue.
And afterward, I-- I did have a dilemma in believing.
But--
After the war.
When I lost everybody, I-- I have big problems.
Before the war, I was 16.
And I-- I-- I did after my mother, what my mother was believing, and the tradition, and these Jewish rules she was keeping.
I and my sister, we went after her.
We did the same thing.
I-- I went to public school.
Seven years I have in-- in Zakopane.
First, in Szczawnica, five years, and then two years in-- in Zakopane.
And then I start to learn how to sew from my father, to help him.
And also with the souvenir stuff I help him.
So I went to evening school to-- to get some more education.
And I went in this school until war broke out.
When the war broke out, we couldn't go to school.
Was not allowed.
Before the war, did you belong to any youth organizations?
Yes, I did.
To Zionistic organizations I belonged.
It was like Scouts, Jewish Scouts.
And the-- the purpose was to-- to go to Palestine, to Israel.
I was-- well, they try educate us and teach us Hebrew, alefbet and words.
Not too much, but the basics.
And they took us to the nature, and skiing, and to what else they-- was very, very nice leaders being there.
The main leader, his name was Moniek Spirra.
And he emigrated to Israel.
Moniek is Polish-- is Polish and is like Moshe.
Moshe Spirra, S-P-I-R-RA.
Spirra.
I met him in Israel, and he still was very dedicated Zionist.
Yes.
After war.
But he saved his life because before the war, he emigrated to Israel.
They were younger.
My sister, she was three years younger.
And my brother, he was '27, five years younger.
And just they went to school.
They'd been good students, nice people, good children.
No, it was not very much.
This was like 30 families-- Jewish families-- was there.
And it was mixed.
It was Conservatives, and some Orthodox Jews had been there.
And everybody died.
Yeah.
How we'd been treated?
You could feel antisemitism there.
You-- you could see sometimes they were waiting in front of Jew store.
And they say, do not go in.
This is a Jewish store.
Or they broke windows sometimes.
One time happened they went-- the older Jews praying early in the morning before High Holy Days.
And they beat them up very bad.
A few years.
Maybe two, three years before war it was.
We knew it was antisemitism there.
And even small children, they went on the street when they saw us.
And they say, [POLISH].
Jewish-- you're Jewish.
You go to Palestine.
You don't belong here.
Stuff like this we experienced.
No regular basis.
Sometimes happens.
Sometimes.
Polish people, they'd been a lot antisemitic.
They didn't help in the war, [INAUDIBLE] somebody.
Yeah.
Her father, he died young.
Was a tragedy.
He went to-- to the woods, cut trees, and one tree killed him.
And he was young.
Her father, a tree fell on him.
And he was a very religious Jew, what I heard.
I-- I even didn't know him.
And my grandmother, [POINTS], here is on the picture.
She wore a sheitel [INAUDIBLE] a week.
She was so religious.
So my mother, she-- she became-- she saw the tradition and everything.
So she was, like, not as much.
But she-- she was a religious woman.
My father's family were-- I don't know how was the family, but he wasn't religious at all.
I-- his father.
I-- I knew his father.
He was a teacher.
And he teach German in school.
And he was a very sweet, sweet man.
I don't know his beliefs.
I was-- I was small at this time.
And I-- I don't know.
But my father, he lost his mother when he was three years old.
She died.
So then they put him to his aunt in Moravska Ostrava in Czech.
And there he went to school.
And he has a very hard life because they'd been bakers.
And in morning, he has to get up, 5 o'clock, and take all the rolls and bread and distribute in the houses.
And after that, he went to school, after three hours of hard work.
Do you know how he and your mother met?
Yeah, I know how they met.
My mother-- in Szczawnica, where I was born, where the minerals water had been, so there my mother had a brother.
And he went there.
The-- the wife of the brother, her sister-in-law, she was cooking for people when it was a big season and the guests came.
So she was cooking lunches and dinners.
And he was eating there, my-- my father.
And my mother, she came to visit his-- his brother.
And she was a very beautiful woman.
You can tell on the picture.
And he saw her in the mirror, and he fell in love right away.
And-- and he-- he started to push her, because he asked her whether she has somebody.
She said yeah.
And he says, I am interested, but in three days I have to have the answer because I'm going to France.
If you tell yes, so I stay here.
So she said yes.
And so in three days, they'd been engaged.
And this was the best marriage what you can imagine.
Never-- people not believe me, but never I heard an argument.
Never in this marriage.
Well, I have lots of memories.
In what field do you mean?
In what--
Just big love and respect and honesty is what I got from my parents.
Well, all-- all we start to feel when-- when the antisemitic stuff started.
And we knew it's an influence-- from somewhere it came.
For example, there was like Olympic Games in Zakopane in summer and in winter.
And they came, the-- the Nazis and their spies.
The spy came.
And they knew everything what is.
If they came in-- in Zakopane, they occupied everything that was important.
Oh, airport and the-- the hotels, the beautiful hotels up in there.
And the offices, they-- they right-- right away jump in and sit there.
And then they say, not right away but later on, maybe around the new year-- this was September.
Around the new year, they say, this is a beautiful city.
We don't want Jews here.
All Jews has to pack and go.
And this was a very terrible moment for my parents.
Three children, and right away pack and go?
Go where to go?
We didn't know.
But we really packed, and my father rented a big truck, put everything in the truck.
And we went to Cracow.
Did he take the-- what did he take with him?
Everything we took with us, all belongings.
The-- the [INAUDIBLE], he has the business.
The souvenir they took from him.
Before, I mentioned that.
Yeah, it was around new year.
When we arrived in Cracow, first thing was to rent some-- some room and stay somewhere.
So in a Jewish very nice family, my father rented a room.
And there we stayed as long as we could.
And then they say it's not enough room and they don't-- they need the room.
So we rented in the outskirts of Cracow.
We rented an apartment.
And happened that this part where we rent the apartment became a ghetto.
So then we don't have to move.
We stayed in this ghetto.
When did it become a ghetto?
Ghetto become probably '41.
He-- he was sewing.
He-- he was a tailor.
He was a very good tailor.
Also for ladies, also for men.
So always he finds job.
And we-- I helped him.
And then I found a job in sewing, and I was working too.
So I helped.
And my mother, she helped him.
Oh, she-- they-- they went to school.
Yeah, they were younger than I was.
It changed dramatically.
We were closed.
They put bricks around.
Was like city in a city.
You couldn't go out.
You-- you have to have ID to go out and in each time, if somebody worked outside.
Probably they-- they were Ukrainian and German.
Some Jews.
Also some Jewish policemen they make from there.
So--
But you have stores there where you can shop in the ghetto.
And we--
I don't know.
I don't know.
A lot.
It was like a-- a part of Cracow.
I suppose a big place.
Many people.
And then they started to make the selection and deportation from this ghetto.
Yeah.
We didn't know.
We didn't-- everybody lived his own life and started to make a living.
And day by day, somehow they made it.
And we would wait until the end if they not take us to concentration camp.
Then they make the concentration camp in Cracow, Prussia.
So we have to leave the ghetto.
No.
No.
Because it was not-- it was a cemetery there.
Jewish cemetery.
Just they-- they create.
It was no problem for them.
So after-- they make a big deportation when they took my mother and my brother.
This I know.
It was October, 1942.
October 19.
Yeah, never I saw them again, never.
Oh, this was a terrible day.
They-- first, they start to scream, the German, early in the morning, with the dogs.
And then they say, everybody has to go out.
And we went out.
And there, when we went out, they make the selections.
And old people on one side, the too young and children and-- with the old people.
And healthy young people on the other side.
So they went for staying, and, well, the small children and sick people, they went for going.
And we knew they go to gas chambers.
We knew.
We-- we knew.
Through-- from mouth to mouth.
It come from the German to the Jewish policemen, and then it comes to the families.
And we were in panic.
It was rumors that there will be deportation.
Was terrible fear there.
Yeah.
We knew.
And my brother, he was blond with blue eyes.
He didn't look like a Jew.
And he wants to go out.
And my mother and father, they'd been scared with a young boy, how he will survive.
So they didn't allow him to go.
If they would allow him, maybe he wouldn't be alive.
And then there was the selection, and they chose him because he was young, a little boy, 14.
How old are you?
He was 14, younger three years than you.
And they took him.
And mother, she was so desperate.
She ran after him.
So I lost both of them.
She didn't know.
Just she ran.
She knew she couldn't be without him.
It was too painful.
So-- so I lost both of them.
No, because we were in another place.
I was staying with my father and my sister.
And they took him, and she ran after.
I don't know where she ran.
It was behind a corner on the place where was the selection.
So we stayed there.
And then was quiet.
And we went back to our-- to our apartment in the ghetto.
They didn't come for me.
We worked in this factory.
We'd been sewing the uniforms.
Yeah, Optima.
And--
For the German.
The uniforms we'd been sewing.
All of-- all three of us had been working in there.
No, and then they say they're going to liquidate the ghetto.
There will be no ghetto no more.
So who was there has to move to a concentration camp.
Well, this was in '42. 1942 year.
And was-- it was fall, before winter.
The fall.
Autumn.
And we-- what we could take, we took with us.
Of course, not furniture, just what we could take in the hands.
Yes.
My sister.
And what is the question?
In the concentration camp?
Oh, before they liquidate?
We have one room.
And the kitchen was shared with three families.
And it was-- was not too bad.
We could exist there if they leave us.
But they liquidate the ghetto, and everybody has to go to the concentration camp in Plaszow.
Well, this was terrible day.
Be without mother and brother, and now you have to go.
Leave everything, just take something in your two hands and go to a concentration camp, which was built on a cemetery.
So this was terrible, but what you can do?
We knew.
Well, they'd been talking.
People had been talking.
And the commandant was Amon Goeth.
And we didn't know how terrible he is.
But then we find out when we move in there.
And when we came there, it was a cemetery, so they gave us--
Oh, we walked there.
Yeah.
It was not so far.
It was not so far from the ghetto.
And--
They give us hammer in the hands and tell us to break the stones, the gravestones, and make street from it, roads, because it was dirty roads.
It was--
This was Jewish cemetery there where they created the-- the concentration camp.
And Amon Goeth, he was our commandant, and he'd been shooting people.
So you can imagine what's-- how scary place it could get there.
First, we'd been breaking the stones.
And then one group they took and told us we're going to a brush factory.
To a brush factory.
And we worked there in the brush-- I worked there in the brush factory.
All kind of brushes.
Cleaning dresses, coats, for shoes.
All--
All kind of brushes.
There in the-- in Plaszow.
Yes.
But what was bad there, that there was window in there.
And from Czechoslovakia they brought transports, and they'd been shooting the people.
And we could see.
Because the window was there.
Behind the window.
First they have to put out all gold what they have.
Gold.
Start with, like, gold and jewelry.
And then they shoot them.
They're from-- from Slovakia they came.
Yeah, from the trucks.
And we have to see this.
